title: Genesis
author: Traditionally attributed to Moses
authorNote: Jewish and Christian tradition credits Moses with writing Genesis, but many modern scholars propose it was compiled over centuries from multiple source traditions (sometimes labeled J, E, and P) and reached its final form after the Babylonian exile. The debate remains unresolved and is a matter of ongoing scholarly discussion rather than settled fact.
dateWritten: Traditionally dated to the 15th-13th century BC (Mosaic authorship); many scholars date the compilation and editing of its sources to between the 10th and 5th centuries BC, with final editing possibly during or after the exile.
audience: The people of Israel, to ground their identity in a shared account of creation, ancestry, and covenant.
purpose: To narrate the origins of the world, humanity, and the nation of Israel, establishing the foundational covenant relationship between God and the patriarchs.
historicalContext: Genesis was composed and transmitted within an ancient Near Eastern culture that shared literary forms (creation and flood stories) with neighboring civilizations like Mesopotamia and Egypt, while presenting a distinct theological outlook. Its final compilation likely responded to the community's need to preserve identity and history during periods of upheaval, including possibly the Babylonian exile.

title: Exodus
author: Traditionally attributed to Moses
authorNote: As with the rest of the Pentateuch, tradition ascribes Exodus to Moses, while many scholars view it as a composite text shaped by multiple sources and later editorial work, with its narrative core reflecting older oral and written traditions.
dateWritten: Traditionally associated with the 15th or 13th century BC, depending on how the Exodus event is dated; many scholars place the text's literary development between the monarchy period and the exile.
audience: The Israelite community, to preserve the memory of liberation from slavery and the giving of the covenant law.
purpose: To recount Israel's deliverance from Egyptian bondage and the establishment of covenant law and worship at Sinai.
historicalContext: The narrative is set against a backdrop of Egyptian imperial power in the Late Bronze Age, though archaeological evidence for the specific events described is debated among historians. The book reflects a community defining its national and religious identity around a formative liberation story.

title: Psalms
author: A collection attributed to multiple authors, including David, Asaph, the sons of Korah, and others
authorNote: Psalms is an anthology rather than a single-author work; roughly half the psalms carry a Davidic superscription, though scholars debate whether these headings indicate authorship, dedication, or later liturgical attribution. The collection as a whole was compiled and edited over several centuries.
dateWritten: Composed across a broad span from roughly the 10th century BC to the post-exilic period (5th century BC), with final compilation likely completed after the exile.
audience: Worshippers of ancient Israel, used in personal devotion and temple liturgy.
purpose: To give voice to a full range of human experience before God, including praise, lament, thanksgiving, and reflection.
historicalContext: The psalms emerged from centuries of Israelite worship life, spanning the united monarchy, divided kingdom, exile, and post-exilic restoration, and were gradually gathered into five books mirroring the structure of the Torah.

title: Proverbs
author: Traditionally attributed largely to Solomon, with contributions from other sages
authorNote: The book explicitly names Solomon in several headings and also attributes sections to figures such as Agur and King Lemuel, indicating it is a compiled anthology of wisdom sayings rather than the work of one writer, gathered and edited over an extended period.
dateWritten: Traditionally linked to Solomon's reign in the 10th century BC; many scholars believe the collection was compiled and expanded over several centuries, with final editing possibly during or after the exile.
audience: Young people and members of the Israelite community seeking practical guidance for a wise and moral life.
purpose: To instruct readers in practical wisdom, ethical living, and the fear of the Lord as the foundation of knowledge.
historicalContext: Proverbs reflects the broader ancient Near Eastern wisdom literature tradition, sharing forms and themes with Egyptian and Mesopotamian instructional texts, while framing wisdom within Israel's covenant faith.
structure: Words of Lemuel and the Ideal Wife (chapter 31)

title: Ecclesiastes
author: Traditionally attributed to Solomon ('the Preacher' or 'Qoheleth')
authorNote: The book presents its speaker as 'son of David, king in Jerusalem,' which tradition has long identified as Solomon, but many scholars note linguistic features suggesting a later date of composition, with the Solomonic voice functioning as a literary persona rather than a direct authorial claim.
dateWritten: Traditionally dated to Solomon's reign (10th century BC); many scholars, based on the book's Hebrew style, date its composition to the post-exilic period, roughly the 5th-3rd century BC.
audience: Israelite readers grappling with questions of meaning, mortality, and the limits of human achievement.
purpose: To explore the meaning of life and the limitations of human wisdom, wealth, and pleasure when viewed 'under the sun.'
historicalContext: The reflective, philosophical tone of Ecclesiastes has led scholars to compare it with wisdom literature from across the ancient Near East and the Hellenistic world, suggesting a setting where Israelite thought engaged with broader philosophical currents.

title: Isaiah
author: Traditionally attributed to the prophet Isaiah son of Amoz
authorNote: Tradition holds that Isaiah wrote the entire book in the 8th century BC. Many modern scholars argue the book reflects at least two or three distinct historical settings (often called First, Second, and Third Isaiah), suggesting later prophets or disciples writing in Isaiah's tradition contributed later chapters. This remains a significant and unresolved scholarly debate.
dateWritten: Traditionally dated entirely to the 8th century BC; many scholars propose the book took shape over a longer period, from the 8th century through the Babylonian exile and into the post-exilic era (6th-5th century BC).
audience: The kingdom of Judah, facing threats from Assyria and later Babylon, and the exilic and post-exilic community.
purpose: To call Judah to faithfulness amid political crisis and to proclaim both judgment and future hope, including restoration after exile.
historicalContext: The book addresses the turbulent Assyrian threat to Judah in the 8th century BC and, in its later chapters, speaks to the experience of Babylonian exile and the prospect of return, reflecting a period of profound national upheaval and reflection.
structure: Judgment on Judah and the Nations (chapters 1-39)
themes: the coming of a messianic figure

title: Jeremiah
author: Traditionally attributed to the prophet Jeremiah, with material recorded by his scribe Baruch
authorNote: The book itself describes Jeremiah dictating his prophecies to Baruch (Jeremiah 36), lending strong support to its traditional attribution, though scholars note the text's complex transmission history, including a notably different and shorter version preserved in the ancient Greek translation, suggesting editorial development over time.
dateWritten: Composed primarily in the late 7th to early 6th century BC, during Jeremiah's ministry leading up to and following the Babylonian conquest of Jerusalem in 586 BC.
audience: The kingdom of Judah in its final decades before the Babylonian conquest, and the exiled community afterward.
purpose: To warn Judah of impending judgment for covenant unfaithfulness and to offer hope of eventual restoration beyond exile.
historicalContext: Jeremiah prophesied during a period of political turmoil as Judah wavered between alliances with Egypt and Babylon, culminating in Jerusalem's destruction and the exile of its people in 586 BC, an event the book directly addresses.

title: Daniel
author: Traditionally attributed to Daniel, a Judean exile serving in the Babylonian and Persian courts
authorNote: Tradition attributes the book to Daniel himself in the 6th century BC. Many scholars, noting the book's apocalyptic imagery and Aramaic-Hebrew language mix, propose that at least its later visions were composed or edited as late as the 2nd century BC, during the Maccabean period. This dating is a significant point of scholarly debate rather than settled consensus.
dateWritten: Traditionally dated to the 6th century BC, during and shortly after the Babylonian exile; many scholars date the final form, especially chapters 7-12, to the 2nd century BC.
audience: Jewish exiles and later communities facing pressure to abandon their faith under foreign rule.
purpose: To encourage faithfulness to God under foreign domination through stories of courage and visions of God's ultimate sovereignty over history.
historicalContext: The narrative portions are set in the Babylonian and Persian royal courts of the 6th century BC, while the visionary chapters are widely read as addressing later periods of persecution, including the Hellenistic era under the Seleucid ruler Antiochus IV.

title: Matthew
author: Traditionally attributed to the apostle Matthew (Levi), a former tax collector
authorNote: Early church tradition credits the apostle Matthew with this Gospel. Many scholars note its heavy reliance on Mark and a shared sayings source (often called Q), and question whether an eyewitness apostle would have depended so extensively on another author's account, making authorship a genuinely debated question among scholars.
dateWritten: Traditionally dated to the mid-1st century AD; many scholars date it to roughly AD 80-90, after the destruction of the Jerusalem temple in AD 70.
audience: A largely Jewish-Christian audience, given the Gospel's frequent citations of Hebrew scripture and Jewish customs.
purpose: To present Jesus as the promised Messiah who fulfills Hebrew scripture and to instruct the early Christian community in his teaching.
historicalContext: The Gospel was likely composed in the wake of the Jewish-Roman war and the temple's destruction, a period in which the early Christian movement was defining its identity in relation to broader Judaism.
structure: Sermon on the Mount and Ministry (chapters 5-13)
themes: the Sermon on the Mount's ethical teaching

title: Mark
author: Traditionally attributed to John Mark, an associate of Peter and Paul
authorNote: Early church tradition, notably from the 2nd-century bishop Papias, holds that Mark recorded Peter's recollections of Jesus. Most scholars accept this general attribution and, further, regard Mark as likely the earliest Gospel written and a source used by both Matthew and Luke.
dateWritten: Widely dated to around AD 65-75, with tradition often placing it shortly before or after Peter's death in Rome.
audience: Likely a Gentile Christian community, possibly in Rome, facing persecution.
purpose: To present a fast-paced narrative of Jesus's actions and identity as the suffering Son of God, encouraging endurance under hardship.
historicalContext: If composed around the Jewish-Roman war (AD 66-73) or the persecution of Christians under Nero, Mark's emphasis on suffering discipleship would have spoken directly to a community under real threat.

title: Luke
author: Traditionally attributed to Luke, a physician and companion of Paul
authorNote: Tradition, supported by the similar style and stated authorship link between Luke and Acts, attributes both books to Luke, a Gentile physician mentioned in Paul's letters. This attribution is broadly accepted among scholars, though the author's precise identity and relationship to eyewitnesses remains a topic of scholarly interest.
dateWritten: Commonly dated to around AD 80-90, though some scholars argue for an earlier date in the 60s AD; the prologue indicates it drew on earlier written and oral sources.
audience: Addressed to 'Theophilus,' likely representing a Gentile Christian audience seeking an orderly historical account.
purpose: To provide a carefully researched narrative of Jesus's life emphasizing his compassion for the marginalized and the universal scope of salvation.
historicalContext: Written for a broader Greco-Roman audience, Luke situates Jesus's story within world history and Roman political context, reflecting the early church's expansion beyond a purely Jewish setting.
themes: the role of the Holy Spirit

title: John
author: Traditionally attributed to the apostle John, 'the disciple whom Jesus loved'
authorNote: Early tradition identifies the author as the apostle John. Many scholars, noting the Gospel's distinct theological vocabulary and structure compared to the other Gospels, propose it emerged from a 'Johannine community' that preserved and shaped the apostle's testimony, possibly reaching final form through later editing.
dateWritten: Widely dated to around AD 90-100, making it likely the last Gospel written, though some scholars argue for an earlier date.
audience: A Christian community, possibly facing tension with a local synagogue, in need of deepened theological understanding of Jesus's identity.
purpose: To present Jesus as the divine Word made flesh so that readers might believe and find eternal life in his name.
historicalContext: Composed later than the other Gospels, John reflects a period of developing Christological reflection and possible separation between early Christian and Jewish synagogue communities.
structure: Book of Glory: Upper Room and Passion (chapters 13-19)

title: Acts
author: Traditionally attributed to Luke, as a sequel to his Gospel
authorNote: Acts identifies itself as a continuation of an earlier account addressed to Theophilus, matching the Gospel of Luke, and tradition attributes both to Luke, a companion of Paul. This shared authorship is widely, though not universally, accepted among scholars.
dateWritten: Commonly dated to around AD 80-90, closely following the traditional dating of Luke's Gospel; some scholars propose an earlier date in the 60s AD.
audience: The same audience as Luke's Gospel, likely Gentile Christian readers seeking to understand the church's early growth.
purpose: To narrate the spread of the early Christian movement from Jerusalem to Rome, driven by the Holy Spirit, through the ministries of Peter and Paul.
historicalContext: Acts documents the church's expansion within the context of the Roman Empire, including its complex relationship with Jewish communities and Roman authorities across the eastern Mediterranean world.
structure: Paul's Arrest and Journey to Rome (chapters 21-28)
themes: the work of the Holy Spirit

title: Romans
author: Paul the apostle
authorNote: Pauline authorship of Romans is essentially undisputed among scholars, making it one of the most secure attributions in the New Testament.
dateWritten: Widely dated to around AD 56-58, written from Corinth near the end of Paul's third missionary journey.
audience: The Christian community in Rome, a congregation Paul had not yet visited.
purpose: To present a systematic explanation of the gospel, righteousness by faith, and the relationship between Jewish and Gentile believers, in preparation for Paul's planned visit.
historicalContext: Written to a Roman church made up of both Jewish and Gentile Christians, the letter addresses tensions that arose partly from the expulsion and return of Jewish residents (including Jewish Christians) under earlier imperial edicts.
structure: Sin and the Need for Righteousness (chapters 1-3)

title: 1 Corinthians
author: Paul the apostle
authorNote: Pauline authorship of 1 Corinthians is widely accepted among scholars with little serious dispute.
dateWritten: Commonly dated to around AD 53-55, written from Ephesus during Paul's third missionary journey.
audience: The Christian community in Corinth, a diverse and cosmopolitan Greek city.
purpose: To address reports of division, moral confusion, and disorderly worship within the Corinthian church, and to answer questions the congregation had raised in a letter to Paul.
historicalContext: Corinth was a wealthy, commercially active Roman colony known for its cultural diversity and competitive social climate, factors that contributed to the factionalism and ethical challenges Paul addresses.

title: Galatians
author: Paul the apostle
authorNote: Pauline authorship of Galatians is essentially undisputed among scholars.
dateWritten: Scholars debate the date depending on which region 'Galatia' refers to; estimates range from the late 40s AD to the mid-50s AD.
audience: Christian communities in the region of Galatia in Asia Minor, largely Gentile converts.
purpose: To defend the gospel of salvation by faith against teaching that required Gentile converts to adopt Jewish law observance, particularly circumcision.
historicalContext: The letter reflects an early and significant controversy in the church over how Gentile believers related to Jewish law, a debate also addressed at the Jerusalem council described in Acts 15.
structure: Paul's Defense of His Gospel and Authority (chapters 1-2)
Freedom and Life in the Spirit (chapters 5-6)

title: Ephesians
author: Traditionally attributed to Paul the apostle
authorNote: Ephesians is traditionally regarded as one of Paul's 'prison epistles.' A notable number of scholars question direct Pauline authorship, pointing to differences in vocabulary, style, and theological emphasis compared to the undisputed Pauline letters, suggesting it may have been written by a close associate in Paul's name and tradition. This is a genuine and ongoing scholarly discussion.
dateWritten: Traditionally dated to around AD 60-62, during Paul's imprisonment in Rome; scholars who question Pauline authorship sometimes suggest a slightly later date, in the AD 70s-90s.
audience: Likely intended as a circular letter to churches in the region of Ephesus and Asia Minor rather than a single congregation.
purpose: To describe the unity of the church as the body of Christ and to encourage believers toward holy living rooted in that unity.
historicalContext: The letter addresses a church context in Roman Asia Minor where Jewish and Gentile believers needed to understand their shared identity as one new community in Christ.
structure: The Riches of Christ and the Church (chapters 1-3)
The Unity and Growth of the Church (chapter 4)

title: Philippians
author: Paul the apostle
authorNote: Pauline authorship of Philippians is widely accepted among scholars with little serious dispute, though some note the letter may combine what were originally more than one shorter letter of Paul's.
dateWritten: Traditionally dated to around AD 60-62, during one of Paul's imprisonments, likely in Rome, though some scholars suggest an earlier imprisonment in Ephesus.
audience: The Christian community in Philippi, a Roman colony in Macedonia and one of Paul's earliest European congregations.
purpose: To thank the Philippian church for their support, encourage joy and unity despite hardship, and warn against false teaching.
historicalContext: Written from prison, the letter reflects Paul's personal circumstances of confinement while addressing a beloved congregation with whom he had a warm, longstanding relationship.

title: James
author: Traditionally attributed to James, identified as the brother of Jesus and leader of the Jerusalem church
authorNote: Tradition identifies the author as James, a prominent early church leader in Jerusalem. Some scholars have questioned this attribution based on the letter's polished Greek style, suggesting either a later author writing in James's name or a scribe assisting with composition; this remains a point of scholarly discussion rather than settled consensus.
dateWritten: Traditionally dated early, around AD 45-50, which would make it among the earliest New Testament writings; some scholars propose a later date, into the AD 60s or beyond, depending on the authorship question.
audience: Jewish Christians scattered outside Palestine, described as 'the twelve tribes in the dispersion.'
purpose: To offer practical ethical instruction on living out genuine faith through works, endurance, and wise speech.
historicalContext: The letter addresses a Jewish Christian audience navigating economic hardship, social favoritism, and the challenge of demonstrating authentic faith amid everyday trials.

title: Revelation
author: Traditionally attributed to the apostle John
authorNote: Early tradition identifies the author as the apostle John, though the text itself simply names its author 'John.' Some scholars, noting stylistic differences from the Gospel and letters of John, propose a different early Christian figure known as 'John of Patmos,' distinct from the apostle. This remains a genuinely debated question.
dateWritten: Most scholars date it to around AD 95, during the reign of the Roman emperor Domitian; a minority propose an earlier date around AD 68-70, under Nero.
audience: Seven churches in Roman Asia Minor facing pressure to compromise their faith under Roman imperial religion.
purpose: To encourage persecuted and pressured Christian communities with a vision of God's ultimate victory over evil and the promise of a renewed creation.
historicalContext: Written during a period of tension between the early church and Roman imperial cult practices, the book uses apocalyptic imagery common in Jewish literature of the era to address political and spiritual pressures facing its first readers.
structure: The Fall of Babylon and Final Victory (chapters 17-20)
The New Heaven and New Earth (chapters 21-22)
